perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall come into force on the Effective Date and shall automatically expire and terminate 10 year( s) from the Effective Date. This agreement may be terminated by either Party (i) at any time on fifteen (15) days prior written notice to the other Party; and (ii) immediately upon written notice of the other Party’s breach of any term of the Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall come into force on the Effective Date and shall automatically expire and terminate 10 year( s) from the Effective Date. This agreement may be terminated by either Party (i) at any time on five (5) days prior written notice to the other Party; and (ii) immediately upon written notice of the other Party’s breach of any term of the Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for termination to 5 days may be unreasonably short and could violate general principles of contract law requiring reasonable notice, depending on the specific context and jurisdiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of contract law regarding reasonable notice for termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-309(3) states that termination of a contract by one party requires reasonable notification be received by the other party. This is a general principle of contract law. Reducing the notification period to 5 days may be considered unreasonable and thus be a violation of the UCC.

perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the notification period for termination from 15 days to 5 days. The Law Explanation states that UCC § 2-309(3) requires 'reasonable notification' for contract termination, and the LLM suggests that 5 days might be deemed unreasonable. The Perturbation Explanation also argues that a 5-day notice period might be unreasonably short. While the snippets do not directly define what is 'reasonable', the explanation provided implies that the change could contradict the legal requirement for reasonable notification, thus creating a legal contradiction due to the inconsistency.

perturbation.original_text: The confidentiality obl igations set out in this Agreement will survive expiry and/or termination of this Agreement for a period of one (1) yea r.
perturbation.changed_text: The confidentiality obl igations set out in this Agreement will survive expiry and/or termination of this Agreement for a period of ten (10) yea rs.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the confidentiality period to 10 years after termination could be deemed unreasonably long in certain jurisdictions and for certain types of confidential information, particularly if it hinders fair competition or employee mobility, potentially conflicting with laws against unreasonable restraints of trade.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_citation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. A ten-year confidentiality period, particularly if broadly defined, could be seen as an unreasonable restraint of trade if it prevents a party from utilizing general knowledge or skills acquired during the agreement's term, thus conflicting with California law. Though this NDA is unlikely to contradict this law, the length of the ten-year period could come into question.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the confidentiality period to ten years, which the Law Explanation suggests could be seen as an unreasonable restraint of trade under California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The Perturbation Explanation also states that extending the confidentiality period could potentially conflict with laws against unreasonable restraints of trade. The change from one year to ten years significantly increases the potential for the confidentiality agreement to be deemed an unreasonable restraint.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree that breach of this Agreement may result in a Party suffering irreparable harm from the unauthorised use or disclosure of the Confidential Information and may not have an adequate remedy in money or damage s. Accordingly, the Parties will be entitled to seek injunctive relief, and any other r elief permitted by applicable law, from any court of competent jurisdiction to enforce its rights under this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree that breach of this Agreement may result in a Party suffering irreparable harm from the unauthorised use or disclosure of the Confidential Information and may not have an adequate remedy in money or damage s. Accordingly, the Parties will automatically be entitled to seek injunctive relief, regardless of proof of actual harm or irreparable injury.
perturbation.explanation: Stating that parties are 'automatically' entitled to injunctive relief 'regardless of proof of actual harm' contradicts general equitable principles and the requirements for obtaining injunctive relief in most jurisdictions, which mandate demonstrating irreparable harm and the inadequacy of monetary damages. UK Courts require proof of harm for an injunction.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 37 of the Senior Courts Act 1981 grants the High Court the power to grant injunctions, but this power is exercised according to established equitable principles. Courts generally require proof of irreparable harm and inadequacy of other remedies before granting injunctive relief. The changed text, by removing this requirement, contradicts these fundamental principles. Although the case law of the UK is not clearly stated on this page, the first URL provides the act in question. The second URL provides relevant background information on injunctions.

perturbation.justification: The changed text states that parties are 'automatically entitled to seek injunctive relief, regardless of proof of actual harm,' which directly contradicts the established equitable principles requiring proof of irreparable harm and inadequacy of other remedies before granting injunctive relief. While the snippets provided only contain information on the Senior Courts Act 1981, the Law Explanation states the relevance of Section 37 of the Senior Courts Act 1981 to granting injunctions according to established equitable principles, reinforcing that the modified text undermines these principles.
